items.q: What is the difference between weather and climate?
items.correct: Weather is short-term atmospheric conditions at a specific time and place; climate is the long-term (typically 30-year) average of weather patterns in a region.
items.misconception: Weather happens outside and climate happens globally.
items.random_wrong: Weather refers to rain and climate refers to temperature.
items.why: Students treat them as different phenomena rather than different timescales. The key distinction is time — climate is statistical, weather is instantaneous.

items.q: What weather does an anticyclone (high pressure system) bring?
items.correct: Descending air warms and dries, suppressing cloud formation — so anticyclones bring clear, settled weather: hot in summer, cold and frosty in winter.
items.misconception: High pressure brings stormy weather because the air pushes down hard.
items.random_wrong: Anticyclones bring heavy rainfall because they trap moisture.
items.why: Students conflate 'high pressure' with 'strong weather'. In fact, sinking air evaporates clouds — depressions (low pressure with rising air) bring storms, not anticyclones.

items.q: Why can a cold winter NOT disprove climate change?
items.correct: Climate is the long-term statistical average; a single cold winter is a weather event. Climate change is the trend in averages and extremes over decades, not the absence of cold days.
items.misconception: A really cold winter proves global warming is not happening.
items.random_wrong: Climate change only happens in summer.
items.why: This is one of the most common public misconceptions. Climate is the slope of the trend over 30+ years; individual weather events are scatter around that line — both can be cold.

items.q: What weather is associated with an ANTICYCLONE in summer?
items.why: It's Hot, dry, clear skies. “Heavy rain and wind” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: On a synoptic chart, what do CLOSELY PACKED isobars indicate?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Strong winds. “Calm weather” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What is the AVERAGE air pressure at sea level in millibars?
items.why: Exact answer: 1013. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
